nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rocky river rapids; inhabits crevices among boulders, stones, and submerged tree trunks in the Rio Xingu and lower Rio Iriri. Juveniles found at shallower depths (1–3 m); adults occur deeper in turbulent flows.

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and flat rocks forming caves and crevices
ceramic or PVC breeding pipes (15 cm diameter, rough inner surface)
large driftwood as territory markers and refuge for females
fine sand or rounded pea gravel substrate
heavy circulation/powerheads to replicate Xingu current
dim lighting — darkened conditions preferred; very bright light suppresses activity

diet.notes: The Sunshine Pleco leans heavily toward carnivory, and its spoon-shaped teeth are adapted for crushing snails — the dominant food source in the wild. In the aquarium, the diet should emphasize protein: high-quality sinking carnivore or cichlid pellets, frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, tubifex worms, mussels, prawn, and occasional dead fish are all suitable staples. Vegetable matter — blanched zucchini, squash, peas, or spinach — and algae wafers should be offered weekly for nutritional balance. Despite its sucker mouth, the Sunshine Pleco does not subsist on algae alone and should not be relied on as a tank cleaner. Feeding is best done at dusk or after lights-out, as the species is nocturnal.

goodWith: larger South American tetras (e.g., bleeding heart, Colombian)
corydoras (upper water column separation reduces competition)
avoidWith: conspecific males (intense territorial fighting; only one male per tank unless very large)
other large plecos competing for bottom territory
small fish or invertebrates (predation risk — snails actively hunted)
aggressive or nippy cichlids that may harass resting pleco

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image; verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: The Sunshine Pleco (Scobinancistrus aureatus, also known as L14) is a boldly patterned armored catfish from Brazil’s Rio Xingu. Juveniles display brilliant orange spots; adults retain smaller yellow spots on a dark body. Growing to around 29 cm, it requires a large, high-flow aquarium. IUCN Vulnerable.
careNotes:
The Sunshine Pleco (Scobinancistrus aureatus, also catalogued as L14 and L014) is a boldly patterned armored catfish: juveniles display brilliant orange spots and orange-tipped fins on a dark base, and adult coloration shifts to smaller yellow spots as the fish matures. It is endemic to the Rio Xingu in Pará state, Brazil, where it inhabits fast-moving rocky rapids with very high dissolved oxygen.

A minimum tank of 450 L (approximately 120 US gallons) with a footprint of at least 180 cm is required for a single adult; the species produces substantial waste and demands correspondingly powerful filtration and turnover. Replicate the natural environment with multiple powerheads creating strong current, smooth boulders forming tight crevices, and very dim or absent overhead lighting. Bright light suppresses activity and is generally considered a source of chronic stress.

Water quality is paramount: the Xingu runs warm (26–30°C), soft, and acidic. Maintain temperature in the 26–30°C range; pH 6.0–7.0 is ideal, with an upper tolerance of around 7.2. Water hardness should be low (gH 1–8, kH 1–6). Weekly 30–50% water changes are strongly recommended, as the species is sensitive to ammonia and nitrate accumulation.

The Sunshine Pleco leans heavily toward carnivory, with spoon-shaped teeth adapted for crushing snails — the primary natural prey. In captivity, a protein-rich diet of sinking carnivore pellets, frozen bloodworms, mussels, shrimp, and tubifex worms should form the staple, supplemented weekly with blanched vegetables and algae wafers. Feed after lights-out to match the species' nocturnal activity patterns.

Males tend to be intensely territorial with one another; only one male should be kept per standard aquarium. Tank-raised F1 specimens have become the preferred source since Brazil’s temporary export ban (Portaria MMA nº 445/2014, later modified by Portaria MMA nº 130/2018 allowing export under a recovery plan), and captive-bred individuals are widely reported to be more adaptable. The species is IUCN Vulnerable (2022), with an estimated population decline of more than 30% over 35 years attributed to the Belo Monte hydroelectric dam flooding approximately 34% of its confirmed range in the Volta Grande do Xingu stretch.
breedingNotes:
The Sunshine Pleco is a cave-brooding species; successful captive spawning requires a very large, well-maintained aquarium (250+ gallons reported), extreme water quality, and near-total darkness during the conditioning and breeding period. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 3–5 years. Breeding is triggered by simulating the Amazonian rainy season: perform frequent, large (50%) water changes with slightly cooler, softer water to replicate seasonal dilution and temperature drop. Strong water current and high dissolved oxygen are essential throughout.

Provide multiple cave options — ceramic or PVC pipes with roughened interior surfaces (approximately 15 cm diameter) placed at tank corners. The male selects and defends a cave, attracting females. Clutch size is approximately 100–150 large orange eggs (2–4 mm). The male guards the eggs and newly hatched fry; incubation is 7–10 days at 28°C, with fry remaining in the cave for a further 10–12 days. Fry may be fed crushed algae wafers and fine protein foods from first emergence. The species has been successfully bred by dedicated hobbyists but is considered challenging due to space, water change regimen, and conditioning time required.

primary.name: FishBase — Scobinancistrus aureatus
primary.notes: Primary reference. Confirmed max size 29 cm TL (male/unsexed), weight 290 g, taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Xingu River basin, Brazil), IUCN Vulnerable status (2022), freshwater/demersal tropical habitat.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Scobinancistrus aureatus
additional.notes: Page returned 404 at access date; parameters cross-checked from scotcat.com and aquariumsource.com which cite SeriouslyFish data. Temperature 25–29°C, pH 5.8–7.2, mixed protein/vegetable diet, rocky rapids habitat confirmed.

additional.name: Scotcat — Scobinancistrus aureatus
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 25–29°C, pH 5.8–7.2, adult size 30 cm, diet (sinking pellets, bloodworm, dead fish, mussels, prawns), native range (Rio Xingu and Para rivers, type locality Ihla da Fazenda). Noted IUCN Vulnerable with Belo Monte dam threatening ~34% of confirmed range.

additional.name: AMAZONAS Magazine — Successful Breeding of the Sunshine Pleco
additional.notes: Detailed first-hand captive breeding account. Tank 250 gal / 960 L (200 × 80 × 70 cm); breeding water 28°C, pH 7.5, gH 4°dGH; 50% water changes several times weekly; darkened room essential; ceramic pipes with roughened interior. Diet: Hikari Algae Wafers, high-protein pellets, boiled snails, weekly squash.

additional.name: Aquarium Source — Sunshine Pleco (L014) Care, Size, Lifespan, Breeding
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Confirmed temperature 74–84°F (23–29°C), pH 6.0–7.2, hardness up to 30 dKH, minimum 120–125 gal tank, lifespan 10–15 years, omnivore diet (protein staple + weekly vegetables), cave breeder, 100–150 eggs, 7–10 day incubation, male guards fry.

fish.breedingNotes: The Sunshine Pleco is a cave-brooding species that reaches sexual maturity at approximately 3–5 years. Successful captive spawning requires a very large aquarium (250 gallons or more), near-total darkness, and a heavy water change schedule that simulates the Amazonian rainy season. The male guards a clutch of roughly 100–150 orange eggs, with incubation taking 7–10 days at temperature. Captive breeding has been achieved by dedicated hobbyists but is considered challenging and uncommon outside specialist fishrooms.
